title: Robotic Arm Controller
description: Program a robotic arm (real or simulated) to perform pick-and-place operations. Implement forward and inverse kinematics, trajectory planning, and a simple gripper control system. This project bridges the gap between kinematics theory and practical robot programming.
whyItMatters: Robot arms are the foundation of industrial automation — every factory robot that welds, paints, assembles, or packages uses the same kinematic principles you will implement in this project. Understanding how to command a robot arm through Cartesian space, plan smooth trajectories, and coordinate gripper actions is a core competency for any robotics engineer.
skillsLearned: Robot simulation with Gazebo or a web-based simulator
prerequisites: Robot kinematics theory (DH parameters, transformation matrices)

steps.title: Define the robot arm model
steps.description: Choose a robot arm to work with — either a physical arm (e.g. a low-cost 6-DOF hobby arm) or a simulated one (e.g. a UR5e in Gazebo or the Peter Corke Robotics Toolbox). Define the DH parameters for each joint. Implement forward kinematics: given a set of joint angles, compute the end-effector position and orientation using homogeneous transformation matrices.
steps.tips: Start with a simulated arm to avoid hardware issues while learning. The Peter Corke Robotics Toolbox for Python is excellent for prototyping kinematics code.

steps.title: Implement inverse kinematics
steps.description: Implement an IK solver for your arm. For a 6-DOF arm with a spherical wrist, derive the analytical closed-form solution. For other configurations, implement a numerical solver (Jacobian pseudoinverse method). Test by computing IK for a target pose, then verifying with FK that the result reaches the target.
steps.tips: Handle multiple IK solutions by selecting the one closest to the current joint configuration. Implement joint limit checking to reject infeasible solutions.

steps.title: Plan and execute trajectories
steps.description: Implement trajectory generation between two poses. Start with joint-space interpolation (linearly interpolate joint angles between start and goal). Then implement Cartesian-space interpolation (the end effector moves in a straight line while the IK solver computes joint angles at each step). Add velocity profiling — trapezoidal or S-curve — for smooth acceleration and deceleration.
steps.tips: Sample the trajectory at a fixed rate (e.g. 50 Hz) and send waypoints to the controller at that rate. Check for singularities along Cartesian paths.

steps.title: Implement pick-and-place logic
steps.description: Define a pick-and-place sequence: move to pre-pick position (above the object), descend to pick position, close gripper, ascend with object, move to place position, descend, open gripper, retract. Implement this as a state machine with transitions gated by position reached and gripper confirmation.
steps.tips: Use approach and retreat motions (vertical moves before and after grasping) to avoid collisions with surrounding objects.

steps.title: Test with multiple objects
steps.description: Set up a workspace with at least 3 objects at known positions. Program the arm to pick each object and place it at a designated target location. Measure placement accuracy — how close is the actual placement to the commanded position? Identify and correct sources of error.
steps.tips: In simulation, add small random perturbations to object positions to test robustness. On real hardware, calibrate the workspace coordinate frame carefully.

deliverables: Working FK and IK implementations with unit tests
Trajectory planner with smooth velocity profiles
Pick-and-place demonstration (video) with at least 3 objects
Technical report documenting the kinematic model, IK solution approach, and measured accuracy

rubric.description: FK and IK produce accurate results verified by cross-checking (FK of IK solution matches target within 1mm)
End effector follows smooth paths without jerky motions or velocity discontinuities
Successfully picks and places objects in at least 90% of attempts
System handles IK failures, singularities, and joint limits gracefully
Clear technical report with kinematic model derivation, code architecture, and test results
